papers.title: Peroxisomal interactome mapping enables network-based modelling of function and disease.
papers.abstract: Peroxisomal dysfunction contributes to a broad spectrum of multisystem disorders, yet mechanistic understanding and therapeutic options remain limited, posing significant challenges for clinical management. Network-based computational strategies support hypothesis generation, biomarker discovery, and drug repurposing, but their usage is constrained by incomplete human interactome coverage-especially by scarcity of high-confidence protein-protein interaction (PPI) data for peroxisomal proteins. We present the first comprehensive map of the peroxisomal interactome, generated using an automated,
papers.summary: This study presents the first comprehensive map of the peroxisomal protein-protein interaction network, generated using an automated approach. The resource enables network-based modeling to better understand peroxisomal function and its role in human disease, potentially aiding hypothesis generation and drug repurposing.

papers.title: Exploration and experimental validation of oxidative stress-related diagnostic genes in interstitial cystitis based on transcriptomics.
papers.abstract: Interstitial cystitis (IC) is a chronic pain syndrome with an elusive diagnosis and poorly understood pathogenesis, in which oxidative stress (OS) is increasingly implicated. This study aimed to identify and validate OS-related diagnostic biomarkers for IC using an integrative computational and experimental approach.
papers.summary: This study identifies and validates oxidative stress-related genes as potential diagnostic biomarkers for interstitial cystitis (IC), a chronic pain condition with unclear causes. Using transcriptomic data and experimental validation, the authors pinpoint specific genes that could aid in diagnosing IC and highlight the role of oxidative stress in its development.

papers.title: Computational identification of putative GyrA and AcrR inhibitors from gut microbiota and natural products targeting multidrug-resistant Salmonella enterica.
papers.abstract: The emergence of multidrug-resistant (MDR) Salmonella enterica poses a critical threat to global health due to its capacity to evade conventional antibiotic therapies. In this study, we employed an integrated in silico strategy combining network pharmacology, molecular docking, ADMET profiling, and molecular simulations to explore diverse metabolites from gut microbiota and natural products as potential inhibitors. Antibiotic resistance genes were retrieved from the Comprehensive Antibiotic Resistance Database and screened via the Antibiotic Resistance Ontology, followed by essentiality analys
papers.summary: This study uses computational methods to identify potential inhibitors from gut microbiota and natural products that target multidrug-resistant Salmonella enterica. By combining network pharmacology, molecular docking, and simulations, the researchers pinpointed compounds that may inhibit key resistance proteins GyrA and AcrR.

papers.title: The cell-type specific interaction based drug repurposing for psychiatric disorders.
papers.abstract: Psychiatric disorders severely challenge the public health, but the development of new psychotropic medications cannot keep pace with clinical needs. The rapid accumulation of single-cell RNA data provides opportunities to develop new computational approaches of drug repurposing. However, there is a lack of dedicated computational tools for psychiatric disorders in this field. In this work, we developed a cell-type specific interaction based computational pipeline scPsyDrug to prioritize the drugs for psychiatric disorders. This tool integrated the single-cell transcriptomics, protein-protein
papers.summary: This paper presents scPsyDrug, a computational pipeline that uses single-cell RNA sequencing data and protein-protein interaction networks to prioritize drug repurposing candidates for psychiatric disorders in a cell-type-specific manner. The tool addresses the lack of dedicated computational approaches for psychiatric drug discovery by integrating single-cell transcriptomics with drug-target interactions.

papers.title: DeepMech: a machine learning framework for chemical reaction mechanism prediction.
papers.abstract: The ability to predict the complete, step-by-step mechanism of chemical reactions from first principles remains a grand challenge in science. The importance of chemical reaction mechanisms (CRMs) pervades almost all domains such as prebiotic chemistry, drug discovery, materials science and so on. Uncovering CRMs remains a complex task, traditionally reliant on expert-driven experiments or expensive quantum chemical computations. While deep learning (DL) studies have shown promise in predicting reaction outcomes, they have largely ignored important intermediates and mechanistic steps en route t
papers.summary: DeepMech is a new machine learning framework that can predict the complete step-by-step mechanism of chemical reactions, including intermediates and mechanistic steps that previous deep learning approaches have largely ignored. This addresses a major challenge in chemistry by moving beyond just predicting reaction outcomes to understanding the full reaction pathway, which has broad applications in drug discovery, materials science, and prebiotic chemistry.

papers.title: Mapping the intellectual landscape of malaria drug repurposing: a systematic analysis of the 51 most cited studies.
papers.abstract: The escalating threat of Plasmodium falciparum resistance to artemisinin-based therapies necessitates accelerated drug development. Drug repurposing offers a promising pathway to bypass the extensive time and cost of de novo discovery.
papers.summary: This paper systematically analyzes the 51 most-cited studies on repurposing existing drugs for malaria treatment, highlighting this strategy as a faster and cheaper alternative to developing new drugs from scratch. The analysis maps the intellectual landscape of the field, identifying key research trends and influential works. This is important because drug resistance to current therapies is a growing threat.

papers.title: Insight into the reactivity and antitumoral potential of allyl rhodanine derivatives: Azomethine ylide cycloadditions, molecular docking, and SAR studies.
papers.abstract: In this work, a series of N-allyl-5-arylidene-rhodanine derivatives was prepared and their reactivity evaluated as dipolarophiles in 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition reactions. Cycloadducts of series 5 were obtained using sarcosine and 5-nitroisatin as precursors, while reactions with sarcosine and 2-pyridinecarboxaldehyde afforded new 2-pyridyl-substituted spiro derivatives (series 7). All compounds from both series were fully characterized and screened for their in vitro anticancer activity against the following five human cancer cell lines: A2780 (ovarian carcinoma), A549 (lung adenocarcinoma), MD
papers.summary: This study synthesized and characterized new rhodanine derivatives, evaluating their anticancer activity against five human cancer cell lines. The compounds showed potential antitumoral effects, with molecular docking and SAR studies providing insights into their reactivity and mechanism of action.

papers.title: Tetrazolo[1,5-c]quinazolines as anti-Klebsiella pneumoniae scaffolds: Bridging in vitro activity and multi-target docking prediction.
papers.abstract: The emergence of carbapenem-resistant Klebsiella pneumoniae (CRKP) represents a significant challenge in antimicrobial chemotherapy, with mortality rates reaching 50% in bloodstream infections. Building on prior disk diffusion evidence of K. pneumoniae inhibition by N-acetamide tetrazoloquinazoline compounds and a cross-series analysis of >60 published derivatives across seven structural subclasses, this study presents the first MIC-quantified antibacterial evaluation against a clinically isolated, multidrug-resistant K. pneumoniae strain. Twenty-one synthetic tetrazolo[1,5-c]quinazoline deriv
papers.summary: This study reports the first quantitative antibacterial evaluation of tetrazolo[1,5-c]quinazoline derivatives against a multidrug-resistant Klebsiella pneumoniae strain, identifying compounds with MIC values as low as 8 µg/mL. The authors also performed multi-target docking predictions to explore potential mechanisms of action. This work provides a promising scaffold for developing new antibiotics against carbapenem-resistant K. pneumoniae.

papers.title: Prioritising search for virtual screening via preliminary interpretable low-feature likelihood-based rankings of drug-target activity measures.
papers.abstract: Current AI-based Virtual Screening (VS) methods seek to manage ultra-large molecular libraries. To this end, they develop increasingly efficient heuristics to rank ligands by their predicted activity against a target protein. However, these methods remain computationally demanding due to the billion-scale compound libraries that must be evaluated without prior, informed guidance.
papers.summary: This paper introduces a preliminary interpretable low-feature likelihood-based ranking method to prioritize compounds for virtual screening, reducing the computational burden of evaluating billion-scale libraries. The approach uses simple, interpretable features to generate initial rankings of drug-target activity, enabling more efficient downstream AI-based screening. This work offers a practical heuristic to guide virtual screening efforts before applying more complex and resource-intensive models.

papers.title: The role of AI-assisted drug repurposing in neurological disorders: a systematic review of validation strategies, challenges and opportunities.
papers.abstract: Neurological disorders refer to a diverse group of conditions that affect the brain, peripheral nerves, and spinal cord and impair socioemotional, cognitive, motor, and sensory functions. Alzheimer's disease (AD), Multiple Sclerosis (MS), Parkinson's disease (PD), Huntington's disease (HD), and Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) are some of the well-known neurodegenerative diseases that affect millions of people worldwide. Despite the advanced technologies and nano-drug delivery systems, the success rate of developing drugs for neurological disorders is significantly low. Among several constr
papers.summary: This systematic review examines how AI-assisted drug repurposing can accelerate the discovery of treatments for neurological disorders like Alzheimer's, Parkinson's, and ALS. It evaluates validation strategies, identifies key challenges such as data quality and model interpretability, and highlights opportunities for integrating AI with nano-drug delivery systems to improve drug development success rates.

papers.title: AI-guided identification of natural CTSL inhibitors with therapeutic potential for renal injury.
papers.abstract: Cathepsin L (CTSL) is a prominent therapeutic target for kidney injury, yet clinically available CTSL inhibitors remain limited. Here, we developed an artificial intelligence (AI)-assisted discovery strategy to identify novel CTSL inhibitors from a natural products library. Through a robust deep learning model and molecular docking, we screened 200 molecules from natural products library for experimental validation. Active candidates were further analyzed by molecular dynamics simulations to characterize binding modes and key CTSL-ligand interaction networks, followed by evaluation of therapeu
papers.summary: Researchers used a deep learning model and molecular docking to screen natural products for inhibitors of Cathepsin L, a target for kidney injury. They identified active candidates and characterized their binding modes, offering a new AI-guided approach to finding potential therapeutics.

papers.title: DRIVE: a comprehensive resource deciphering drug-induced transcriptomic and splicing response in cancer cell.
papers.abstract: Pharmacotherapy induces complex molecular reprogramming in cancer, driving transcriptome-wide alterations and widespread dysregulation of alternative splicing. Despite these profound changes, there remain limited resources characterizing drug-induced whole-transcriptomic responses in cancer. Furthermore, while aberrant splicing can generate immunogenic neoantigens, existing resources fail to systematically integrate drug perturbations, splicing dynamics, and neoantigen landscapes. To address this gap, the DRIVE database was constructed as a comprehensive resource detailing drug-induced transcr
papers.summary: The DRIVE database is a new resource that catalogs how drugs alter gene expression and splicing in cancer cells, including the potential to generate immunogenic neoantigens. It fills a gap by systematically integrating drug perturbations with transcriptomic and splicing data, offering a tool for understanding pharmacotherapy responses and identifying new therapeutic targets.

papers.title: Evaluation of Human-Approved Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients Against Lactate Dehydrogenase from Theileria annulata.
papers.abstract: BackgroundTheileriosis, a tick-borne disease caused by Theileria annulata, leads to global economic losses in livestock, emphasizing an urgent need for the identification of new therapeutic agents with alternative mechanisms of action due to rising buparvaquone resistance combined with climate-driven spread. T. annulata lactate dehydrogenase (TaLDH) has been selected as a promising target in the current study. As approximately half of the FDA-approved veterinary drugs are shared with human medicine, integrating drug repurposing has gained importance as an emerging strategy for the management o
papers.summary: This study evaluates human-approved drugs as potential treatments for theileriosis, a tick-borne disease in livestock, by targeting the lactate dehydrogenase enzyme of Theileria annulata. The researchers identified several existing pharmaceuticals that could be repurposed to combat this parasite, which is becoming resistant to current treatments and spreading due to climate change.

papers.title: Molecular design beyond training data with novel extended objective functionals of generative AI models driven by quantum annealing computer.
papers.abstract: Deep generative modeling to stochastically design small molecules is an emerging technology for accelerating drug discovery and development. However, one major issue in molecular generative models is their lower frequency of drug-like compounds. To solve this problem, we develop a novel, quantum annealing, generative-model approach for optimizing deep generative models, an approach that includes integrating with a D-Wave annealing quantum computer. Of particular note, our neural hash function (NHF) is used simultaneously as a regularization scheme and binarization scheme, of which the latter i
papers.summary: This paper introduces a quantum annealing-based approach to optimize deep generative models for molecular design, addressing the issue of low frequency of drug-like compounds. The method integrates a D-Wave quantum computer with a neural hash function that serves both as regularization and binarization, enabling the generation of molecules beyond the training data.

papers.title: The use of artificial intelligence (AI) in neuropsychiatric drug discovery: current challenges and future directions.
papers.abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) has emerged as a powerful tool for solving real world problems across a wide range of industries and is increasingly being utilised by pharmaceutical companies to discover novel drug targets, biomarkers, and new drugs. Several AI-driven small molecules have entered clinical trials over the past few years, but their fate remains unknown. Currently, no commercially available compounds have been developed solely using AI approaches. In this perspective, we examine the current use of AI in drug discovery for neuropsychiatry. The pace of drug discovery in neuropsychiatr
papers.summary: This perspective article examines the current use of artificial intelligence in neuropsychiatric drug discovery, noting that while AI has identified several small molecules now in clinical trials, no commercially available drugs have been developed solely through AI approaches. The authors discuss the unique challenges and future directions for applying AI to this field, highlighting the slower pace of drug discovery in neuropsychiatry compared to other therapeutic areas.

papers.title: AlphaFold-based peptide structure prediction: Opportunities, limitations, and future directions.
papers.abstract: Accurate prediction of peptide structures and peptide-receptor complexes is essential for rational peptide drug development. However, the inherent conformational flexibility of short and disordered peptides presents a fundamental challenge. The AlphaFold model series, which has progressed from AlphaFold2 through AlphaFold-Multimer to AlphaFold3, has substantially advanced computational peptide structure prediction through innovations in geometric reasoning (invariant point attention) and interface-focused confidence metrics (ipTM score), achieving high accuracy for both monomeric peptide struc
papers.summary: This review examines how AlphaFold models (AlphaFold2, AlphaFold-Multimer, and AlphaFold3) have improved peptide structure prediction through geometric reasoning and interface confidence metrics, achieving high accuracy for peptide-receptor complexes. However, the inherent flexibility of short, disordered peptides remains a fundamental challenge for these methods. The paper discusses opportunities and limitations of AlphaFold-based approaches for rational peptide drug development.

papers.title: 3Br-MGD: few-shot toxicity prediction with a three-branch deep encoder and meta-learning framework.
papers.abstract: Predicting the toxicity of pharmaceutical compounds remains a major challenge in drug discovery. Early and accurate toxicity assessment is essential for eliminating harmful candidates before costly preclinical and clinical testing, thereby improving patient safety, reducing development costs, and accelerating the drug development process. Despite advances in computational toxicology, existing methods often struggle to capture complex molecular characteristics and maintain robust performance under limited-data conditions. To address these challenges, we propose 3Br-MGD, a novel three-branch fra
papers.summary: This paper introduces 3Br-MGD, a deep learning framework that combines a three-branch encoder with meta-learning to predict compound toxicity from limited data. The method aims to improve early toxicity screening in drug discovery by better capturing molecular features and maintaining performance when training data is scarce.

papers.title: Decoding cytokine interactions for psoriasis therapy through computational repurposing of antihypertensive drugs.
papers.abstract: Psoriasis is a chronic, immune-mediated skin disorder characterized by excessive inflammation and the overexpression of pro-inflammatory cytokines, including IL-6, IL-1β, IL-17 A, and IFN-γ. Despite the availability of targeted therapies, current treatments often entail high costs, limited accessibility, and adverse side effects. Drug repurposing-utilizing approved medications for novel indications-offers a cost-effective strategy to accelerate the development of alternative therapies with established safety profiles. Twelve FDA-approved antihypertensive drugs-including ACE inhibitors, angiote
papers.summary: This study computationally repurposes FDA-approved antihypertensive drugs as potential psoriasis therapies by analyzing their interactions with key inflammatory cytokines. The findings suggest that certain blood pressure medications could be cost-effective alternatives to current psoriasis treatments, which are often expensive and have side effects.

papers.title: AD-LSF: A Multimodal Framework for Drug-Target Interaction Prediction Based on Asymmetric Dynamic Gated Modulation and Adaptive Latent Signal Fusion.
papers.abstract: Accurate prediction of drug-target interactions (DTIs) is fundamental to drug discovery and repurposing. Although deep learning-based multimodal integration has advanced DTI modeling, current approaches remain limited in capturing complex cross-modal nonlinear dependencies and in reconciling distributional heterogeneity across data modalities. To address these limitations, we propose a multimodal DTI prediction framework termed Asymmetric Dynamic Gated Modulation and Adaptive Latent Signal Fusion (AD-LSF), which is built upon asymmetric dynamic gating modulation and adaptive latent signal fusi
papers.summary: This paper introduces AD-LSF, a deep learning framework that improves drug-target interaction prediction by using asymmetric dynamic gating modulation and adaptive latent signal fusion to better capture complex cross-modal relationships and handle data heterogeneity. The method addresses limitations in current multimodal approaches for drug discovery and repurposing.

papers.title: A comprehensive dataset of 32 million pentapeptide structures for high-throughput virtual screening.
papers.abstract: Small peptides are widely used as binders, modulators, and structural motifs, but their conformational flexibility complicates structure-based analysis and high-throughput screening. We present an open dataset of three-dimensional structures for the complete space of canonical amino-acid pentapeptides: 3,200,000 unique sequences with up to 10 conformers per sequence, for a total of 32,000,000 peptide conformers. Structures were generated directly from sequence using an automated workflow built on UCSF ChimeraX for model construction, Reduce for hydrogen placement, and RDKit for conformer gener
papers.summary: Researchers created an open dataset of 32 million 3D structures covering all possible pentapeptide sequences (3.2 million unique sequences with up to 10 conformers each) using an automated computational pipeline. This resource enables high-throughput virtual screening and structure-based analysis of small peptides without requiring experimental structure determination.

papers.title: Discovery of potent low-toxicity antimicrobial peptides through diffusion modeling.
papers.abstract: The rapid emergence of multidrug-resistant bacteria has created an urgent need for improved antimicrobial discovery and screening platforms. Here, we present ARCADIAMP, a generative and virtual screening platform that couples an iterative-learning discrete denoising diffusion probabilistic model with a two-stage Evolutionary Scale Modeling 2 (ESM2)-based antibacterial activity classifier to generate, classify, and prioritize potent AMPs with high activity, low toxicity, and favorable serum stability. Eight of the ten experimentally screened peptide candidates showed antimicrobial activity (MIC
papers.summary: Researchers developed ARCADIAMP, a platform that uses a diffusion model and a protein language model to design new antimicrobial peptides with high activity and low toxicity. Eight out of ten computer-designed peptides showed antimicrobial activity in lab tests, demonstrating the platform's potential for discovering new antibiotics.

papers.title: Design and computational evaluation of E-stilbene-bearing 1,3,4-oxadiazole derivatives as potential tyrosinase inhibitors using DFT, molecular docking and ADMET studies.
papers.abstract: A series of E-stilbene-bearing 1,3,4-oxadiazole derivatives (4a-b and 5a-f) was synthesized via the Mizoroki-Heck reaction and evaluated as inhibitors of tyrosinase, a vital enzyme involved in melanin biosynthesis. The synthesized compounds were structurally confirmed through NMR spectroscopy (1H NMR, 13C NMR), infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and mass spectrometry. Nearly all derivatives exhibited inhibitory effects against tyrosinase with IC50 values ranging from 0.32 ± 0.03 µM to 6.28 ± 0.05 µM. Among them, compounds 5d and 5b illustrated the most significant inhibitory activity, with IC50 va
papers.summary: This study synthesized and tested new chemical compounds as potential inhibitors of tyrosinase, an enzyme key to melanin production. Several compounds showed strong inhibitory activity, with the most potent having IC50 values as low as 0.32 µM, suggesting potential applications in treating hyperpigmentation disorders.

papers.title: Therapeutic potential of Citrus medica L. (cv. 'Liscia' and cv. 'Rugosa') phytocompounds targeting biofilm formation, quorum sensing, and antioxidant defense mechanisms.
papers.abstract: Microbial biofilm, quorum sensing (QS)-mediated virulence and oxidative stress-induced inflammation are interrelated pathological processes that play a crucial role in persistent infections and chronic illnesses. Due to the multifactorially of such processes, isolated target-based approaches to therapy usually do not provide long-term effectiveness. A combined network pharmacology and multi-level computational approach was used in order to clarify the molecular mechanisms underlying the antibiofilm, anti-quorum sensing (anti-QS), and antioxidant activities of thirty-three bioactive compounds f
papers.summary: This study uses network pharmacology and computational modeling to identify bioactive compounds from two Citrus medica varieties that may simultaneously target biofilm formation, quorum sensing, and antioxidant defense mechanisms. The findings suggest these phytocompounds could serve as multi-target therapeutic agents for persistent infections and chronic inflammatory diseases.

papers.title: Network pharmacology and in silico analysis of Azadirachta indica phytoconstituents reveal potential hepatoprotective targets and mechanisms.
papers.abstract: Network pharmacology and bioinformatics approaches may provide valuable insights into pharmacological effects by enabling a system-level understanding of how drugs interact with biological networks rather than single targets. The study aimed to elucidate the therapeutic mechanisms of Azadirachta indica leaf extract (AILE) in hepatotoxicity and hepato-injury (HT/HI) through the identification of pathways and molecular targets via network pharmacology. Phytochemical profiling of AILE was performed by Gas Chromatography-Mass Spectrometry (GC-MS). SMILES structures of identified phytochemicals wer
papers.summary: This study uses network pharmacology and bioinformatics to explore how compounds from neem (Azadirachta indica) leaf extract may protect the liver from injury. By analyzing phytochemical profiles and predicting molecular targets, the authors identify key pathways and mechanisms that could explain the plant's hepatoprotective effects.

papers.title: From Chatbots to Co-Scientists: The Impact of Knowledge-Generating AI (AI 4.0) on Healthcare and Research.
papers.abstract: While artificial intelligence (AI) has developed as a computational concept for 70 years, its transformative impact on biomedical research and healthcare has explosively accelerated over just the last five years. We are now standing at the precipice of a profound paradigm shift: The emergence of fourth-generation, knowledge-generating AI (AI 4.0). Unlike its predecessors, which primarily synthesized existing information, AI 4.0 possesses advanced reasoning and multi-agentic capabilities that elevate it from a passive tool to an autonomous "co-scientist." By independently formulating novel, tes
papers.summary: This perspective article argues that a new generation of AI (AI 4.0) with advanced reasoning and multi-agent capabilities is emerging, transforming AI from a passive tool into an autonomous 'co-scientist' that can independently formulate and test novel hypotheses in biomedical research and healthcare. The authors describe how this paradigm shift moves beyond synthesizing existing information to generating new knowledge, potentially accelerating scientific discovery.

papers.title: A benchmark dataset and interpretable deep learning framework for drug-induced developmental neurotoxicity prediction.
papers.abstract: Developmental neurotoxicity (DNT) represents a critical yet underevaluated toxicity endpoint within current chemical safety assessment frameworks, particularly in the context of drug development. In the present study, we constructed a standardized benchmark dataset comprising 2724 structurally curated compounds, including 1362 DNT-positive chemicals derived from the Developmental Neurotox Reference List (DNTREF) of the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and 1362 property-matched presumed negative compounds (non-neuroactive drug-like compounds) selected from the ChEMBL database. Using t
papers.summary: This study created a benchmark dataset of 2,724 compounds for predicting drug-induced developmental neurotoxicity (DNT) and developed an interpretable deep learning framework to classify DNT-positive versus negative chemicals. The dataset combines EPA DNT reference chemicals with property-matched non-neuroactive compounds from ChEMBL, enabling more reliable model training and evaluation.

papers.title: ScrambleBench: a workflow for comparative assessment of structure-based de novo generative models.
papers.abstract: Generative artificial intelligence (AI) has rapidly advanced over the past decade in the field of drug discovery, particularly for the de novo design of small molecules based on target protein structures. While generative AI has the potential to complement traditional structure-based drug design (SBDD) to discover novel hits, their performance is often assessed using non-standardized evaluation criteria. As the number of generative AI models continue to grow, it becomes increasingly important to determine whether these tools are sufficiently robust and reliable for integration into medicinal c
papers.summary: This paper introduces ScrambleBench, a standardized workflow for comparing structure-based generative AI models used in drug discovery. It addresses the lack of consistent evaluation criteria for these models, which is crucial for determining their reliability in medicinal chemistry.

papers.title: Smiles-based bioactivity prediction through molecular encoder selection and data augmentation.
papers.abstract: Quantitative prediction of inhibitor potency can accelerate early-stage drug discovery. Recently, data-driven approaches have gained widespread interest in drug discovery, as evidenced by a growing number of benchmarking challenges and open competitions. In this context, we developed a machine learning-based methodology that can find the most effective way of predicting IC50 values against ASK1 from SMILES, for "Jump AI(.py) 2025: 3rd AI Drug Discovery Competition", hosted by the Korea Pharmaceutical and Bio-Pharma Manufacturers Association (KPBMA) on the Dacon platform. Applying our methodolo
papers.summary: This study presents a machine learning approach to predict inhibitor potency (IC50 values) against ASK1 from SMILES strings, developed for a drug discovery competition. The method focuses on selecting optimal molecular encoders and using data augmentation to improve prediction accuracy.

papers.title: Unveiling acetamide coupled benzothiazole hydrazones as potential anti-proliferative agents: an experimental and computational approach.
papers.abstract: A series of benzothiazole acetamide hydrazones (8a-p) was synthesized and evaluated for anti-proliferative activity against various human cancer cell lines, including a pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (CAPAN-1), a colorectal carcinoma (HCT-116), a leukemia (HL60), a glioblastoma (LN229), a T lymphoblast (Molt-4), a non-small cell lung cancer (NCI-H460), and a B lymphoblast (Z138) cell line. Among the tested derivatives, compounds 8l, 8n, and 8o emerged as lead compounds, exhibiting cytotoxicity across multiple cell lines. Notably, compound 8o demonstrated selective cytotoxicity towards Z138 a
papers.summary: Researchers synthesized a series of benzothiazole acetamide hydrazones and tested them against seven human cancer cell lines. Three compounds showed broad anti-proliferative activity, with one compound (8o) demonstrating selective toxicity toward a B lymphoblast cell line. This study combines experimental synthesis with computational analysis to identify potential new anti-cancer agents.

papers.title: Multiscale systems modelling of communication networks in brain metastasis.
papers.abstract: Brain metastasis remains a major clinical challenge because intracranial progression and therapeutic resistance arise not only from tumour-intrinsic programmes but also from coordinated communication across molecular, cellular, spatial and systemic scales. In this Review, we integrate findings from spatial transcriptomics/proteomics, single-cell and multimodal omics, and computational systems modelling to frame brain metastasis as a multiscale communication disorder in which immune, glial, vascular and systemic networks collectively shape tumour-permissive ecosystems. We discuss how network-ba
papers.summary: This review frames brain metastasis as a multiscale communication disorder, integrating spatial transcriptomics, single-cell omics, and computational modeling to show how immune, glial, vascular, and systemic networks collectively create tumor-permissive ecosystems. It highlights how network-based approaches can reveal new therapeutic vulnerabilities beyond tumor-intrinsic programs.

papers.title: A multi-fidelity tabular prior-data fitted network model for accurate prediction and uncertainty quantification.
papers.abstract: Accurate prediction of unknown labels from feature-label datasets using machine learning is critical for applications spanning drug discovery, disease diagnostics, and climate science. However, challenges persist with limited data, high-dimensional inputs, and multi-fidelity scenarios. We developed multi-fidelity tabular prior-data fitted network (MFTabPFN), a general-purpose multi-fidelity model integrating low- and high-fidelity data through a hierarchical transformer architecture to enhance prediction accuracy and uncertainty quantification (UQ). MFTabPFN captures cross-fidelity correlation
papers.summary: Researchers developed MFTabPFN, a machine learning model that combines low- and high-quality data to improve prediction accuracy and uncertainty quantification, especially useful when data is limited or high-dimensional. The model uses a hierarchical transformer architecture to learn relationships between different data fidelities, outperforming existing methods in drug discovery and other applications.

papers.title: SMASH: Screening Molecules Accurately on Small Hardware. Fast, user-friendly, enhanced with a machine learning virtual screening tool.
papers.abstract: In 2025, we released UAM-Ixachi to democratize, simplify, and accelerate molecular docking and virtual screening methods. It is a free, open-source, and user-friendly tool. Building on that work, we present SMASH, which features several upgrades: it predicts binding sites using machine learning, automatically determines titration states, utilizes a Graphics Process Unit for molecular docking, combines machine learning with energy scoring functions, and clusters data using the K-means algorithm. Tests demonstrated that the tool might handle large projects within a reasonable time using local co
papers.summary: SMASH is a free, open-source virtual screening tool that speeds up drug discovery by using machine learning to predict binding sites, automate titration states, and perform GPU-accelerated molecular docking. It combines machine learning with energy scoring and K-means clustering to handle large projects efficiently on local hardware. This makes advanced screening more accessible to researchers without supercomputing resources.

papers.title: Withanolide A inhibits hIAPP aggregation: An In silico, biophysical, and drosophila-based In vivo validation.
papers.abstract: The aberrant aggregation of human islet amyloid polypeptide (hIAPP) or Amylin into toxic oligomers and fibrils leads to pancreatic β-cell dysfunction and progressive cell death, which is a key pathological feature of Type II diabetes mellitus (T2DM). In this study, we adopted a hybrid approach combining virtual screening and molecular dynamics (MD) simulation, with experimental validation, to identify inhibitors of hIAPP aggregation. Herein, we screened 2000 phytoconstituents from natural products using molecular docking, followed by in silico ADMET predictions. Withaferin A and Withanolide A
papers.summary: This study identifies Withanolide A, a natural compound from Ashwagandha, as an inhibitor of hIAPP aggregation—a process linked to pancreatic β-cell death in Type 2 diabetes. Using computational screening, molecular dynamics simulations, and experimental validation in fruit flies, the authors show that Withanolide A reduces toxic amyloid aggregation. The work highlights a potential therapeutic lead for diabetes by targeting amyloid formation.

papers.title: Targeting HsDHODH: Shape and machine-learning guided discovery and structural validation of SARS-CoV-2 antivirals.
papers.abstract: The frequent emergence of novel RNA viruses and the rapid development of viral resistance highlight the urgent need for new antiviral therapies. In this study, we employed an integrated computational pipeline combining shape-based models, machine-learning (ML)-based models, and hotspot analysis to identify novel human dihydroorotate dehydrogenase (HsDHODH) inhibitors. A virtual screening (VS) campaign of the H3D chemical library identified H3D-002856 as the primary hit (IC50 = 2.92 ± 0.07 μM), which subsequently guided a similarity-based hit expansion (Tanimoto coefficient≥0.7) of 17 structura
papers.summary: Researchers used a combination of shape-based modeling, machine learning, and hotspot analysis to discover new inhibitors of the human enzyme HsDHODH as potential antivirals against SARS-CoV-2. A virtual screen identified a primary hit compound, H3D-002856, with an IC50 of 2.92 μM, which was then used to guide the expansion of structurally similar compounds.

papers.title: Advancing bioinformatics with language models: components, applications, and perspectives.
papers.abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are deep learning-based artificial intelligence models that have achieved remarkable success in natural language processing. Typically composed of neural networks with billions of parameters, they are trained on massive unlabeled datasets using self-supervised or semi-supervised learning. Beyond language, LLMs hold immense potential for addressing complex bioinformatics challenges. This review provides a comprehensive overview of transformer-based model applications in genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, drug discovery, and single-cell analysis. We discuss criti
papers.summary: This review provides a comprehensive overview of how large language models (LLMs), originally developed for natural language processing, are being applied to bioinformatics challenges including genomics, transcriptomics, proteomics, drug discovery, and single-cell analysis. It covers the components of transformer-based models and discusses critical considerations for their use in biological research.

papers.title: P2MAT: A machine learning (ML) driven software for Property Prediction of MATerial.
papers.abstract: Accurate prediction of melting points for pure molecules remains a significant challenge in predictive chemistry, with implications across various scientific fields, including materials science, drug discovery, and separations chemistry. Traditional methods, such as group contribution (GC) techniques, have shown limited success due to the complex relationship between molecular structure and melting point. In this study, we present a data-driven machine learning (ML) approach to predict the melting points of organic compounds, leveraging both 2D and 3D molecular descriptors. Our results indicat
papers.summary: This paper introduces P2MAT, a machine learning software that predicts melting points of organic compounds using 2D and 3D molecular descriptors, outperforming traditional group contribution methods. The tool addresses a long-standing challenge in predictive chemistry with applications in materials science and drug discovery.

papers.title: Efficient and valid large molecule generation via self-supervised generative models.
papers.abstract: The realm of molecular design, particularly for large molecules, presents unique challenges and opportunities in drug discovery and materials science. Large molecule design is inherently more complex and less explored compared to designing small molecules, adding significant difficulty in generative modeling. We aim to establish strong baselines for better scalability, efficiency, and generative performance in this domain. We evaluate the scalability and performance of generative AI models, initially effective for small molecule design, in generating large molecules for potential drugs in gene
papers.summary: This paper explores the use of self-supervised generative models for designing large molecules, which are more complex than small molecules and less explored in drug discovery. The authors establish strong baselines for scalability and efficiency, showing that generative AI models originally developed for small molecules can be effectively adapted to generate large molecules for potential gene-targeting drugs.

papers.title: Generative AI for controllable protein sequence design: A survey.
papers.abstract: The design of novel protein sequences with targeted functionalities underpins a central theme in protein engineering, impacting diverse fields such as drug discovery and enzymatic engineering. However, navigating this vast combinatorial search space remains a severe challenge due to time and financial constraints. This scenario is rapidly evolving as the transformative advancements in AI have been propelling the protein design field into a new era. In this survey, we systematically review recent advances in generative AI for controllable protein sequence design. To set the stage, we first outl
papers.summary: This survey reviews recent advances in using generative AI to design novel protein sequences with targeted functions, addressing the challenge of exploring the vast combinatorial space of possible proteins. The authors systematically cover methods that enable controllable protein design, which has implications for drug discovery and enzyme engineering.

papers.title: Commentary on the fundamentals and development of artificial intelligence models in the life sciences and best research practices.
papers.abstract: Machine learning has a long history in the life and natural sciences. In the age of artificial intelligence (AI), both the opportunities and challenges associated with predictive and generative modeling are increasing. To exert significant influence on experimental programs in interdisciplinary research, AI models and their predictions must be demystified as far as possible, and misconceptions regarding what highly complex models can and cannot do must be avoided. Furthermore, it is necessary to evaluate best practices for the use of AI in life science research including drug discovery. This c
papers.summary: This commentary discusses the opportunities and challenges of using AI in life sciences, emphasizing the need to demystify complex models and avoid misconceptions. It highlights best practices for integrating AI into experimental research, particularly in drug discovery.

papers.title: Investigating the anticancer activity of eravacycline in pancreatic cancer via target-based deep learning and experimental validation.
papers.abstract: Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (PDAC) is a highly lethal malignancy with limited therapeutic options. In this study, we introduce a target-based deep learning framework to investigate the anticancer activity of eravacycline (Erav), a United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA)-approved antibacterial agent previously identified in our work as a potential anticancer candidate through computational screening. We developed a novel two-phase in silico yeast-based prediction model to explore potential mechanisms of action, followed by in vitro and in vivo experimental validation. DNA polymera
papers.summary: This study uses a deep learning framework to predict and validate that the FDA-approved antibiotic eravacycline has anticancer activity against pancreatic cancer. The model identified DNA polymerase as a potential target, which was confirmed through experiments in cells and animal models.

papers.title: Amide-functionalized cholic acid hybrids: design, bioactivity, and computational analysis.
papers.abstract: A series of amide-linked cholic acid derivatives (3a-f) was synthesized through an efficient one-pot acyl chloride coupling strategy, yielding structurally diverse hybrids under mild conditions. The antioxidant potential of these derivatives was evaluated using the DPPH radical scavenging assay, with compounds 3b-d exhibiting the most pronounced activity, in an average of 50%. Antibacterial efficacy was investigated against both Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli, revealing preferential activity toward Gram-positive strains, particularly for aromatic amide-bearing analogs. To elucidate
papers.summary: Researchers synthesized a series of amide-linked cholic acid derivatives and tested their antioxidant and antibacterial activities. Some derivatives showed moderate antioxidant activity and preferential antibacterial activity against Gram-positive bacteria. The study is a standard medicinal chemistry synthesis and screening effort.

papers.title: Integrating machine learning and structure-based simulations to prioritise Z4P as a mutation-resilient IRE1α inhibitor for breast cancer.
papers.abstract: Breast cancer is one of the most prevalent and lethal malignancies affecting women globally. The increasing resistance to current therapeutic strategies highlights the need for novel molecular targets. Inositol-requiring enzyme 1 alpha (IRE1α), a key sensor in the unfolded protein response (UPR), has emerged as a promising therapeutic target due to its role in tumour progression and survival. This study employed an integrative in silico approach combining machine learning, molecular docking, and molecular dynamics simulations to identify potent, non-toxic IRE1α inhibitors for breast cancer tre
papers.summary: This study used machine learning and molecular simulations to identify Z4P as a potential drug that could inhibit IRE1α, a protein involved in breast cancer cell survival, even when the protein mutates. The approach offers a way to find cancer drugs that remain effective despite genetic changes in the target.

papers.title: Bridging the phenotype-target gap for molecular generation via multi-objective reinforcement learning.
papers.abstract: The generation of high-quality candidate molecules remains a central challenge in AI-driven drug design. Current phenotype-based and target-based strategies each suffer limitations, either incurring high experimental costs or overlooking system-level cellular responses. To bridge this gap, we propose XMolRL, a novel generative framework that synergistically integrates phenotypic and target-specific cues for de novo molecular generation.
papers.summary: XMolRL is a new AI framework that combines both cellular-level phenotypic data and specific protein target information to generate better drug candidate molecules. By using multi-objective reinforcement learning, it overcomes the limitations of methods that rely on only one type of data, potentially reducing experimental costs while improving molecule quality.

papers.title: Agentomics: an agentic system that autonomously develops novel state-of-the-art solutions for biomedical machine learning tasks.
papers.abstract: Extracting knowledge from biomedical data is crucial for advancing our understanding of biological systems and developing novel therapeutics. The quantity, quality, and resolution of biomedical data constantly evolves, requiring the automation of biomedical machine learning (ML). Existing Automated ML tools lack flexibility, while large language models (LLMs) struggle to consistently deliver reproducible machine learning codebases, and existing LLM Agent-powered solutions lag behind human-engineered ML models.
papers.summary: This paper introduces Agentomics, an AI agent system that autonomously develops state-of-the-art machine learning solutions for biomedical tasks, outperforming both existing automated ML tools and human-engineered models. The system leverages large language models to generate reproducible codebases, addressing key limitations in flexibility and consistency for biomedical data analysis.

papers.title: Bioactivity Assessment and In Silico Target Engagement of Clerodendrum infortunatum Extracts: Correlating Experimental Outcomes With DFT and Docking Predictions.
papers.abstract: Clerodendrum infortunatum (Lamiaceae) is commonly employed in traditional healing practices to manage inflammation, fever, pain, and gastrointestinal ailments. Despite its widespread use, there is limited scientific evidence supporting these therapeutic claims and insufficient characterization of its active phytochemicals. This study aimed to investigate the anti-inflammatory, analgesic, antipyretic, and antidiarrheal properties of the methanolic extract (MECI) and n-hexane fraction (NHF) derived from C. infortunatum leaves through integrated in vivo and in silico methodologies. Anti-inflammat
papers.summary: This study provides experimental evidence supporting the traditional use of Clerodendrum infortunatum for inflammation, pain, fever, and diarrhea by testing leaf extracts in animal models and using computational methods to predict how active compounds might interact with biological targets. The work combines in vivo assays with density functional theory and molecular docking to characterize the plant's therapeutic potential.

papers.title: Artificial Intelligence in skin disease therapeutics: from drug discovery to personalized treatment pathways.
papers.abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) comprises computational methods capable of tasks associated with human cognition, and includes specialized subfields such as machine learning, deep learning, convolutional neural networks for image analysis, and large language models for text-based workflows. In dermatology, these methods are increasingly used across research and clinical practice, supporting drug discovery, diagnosis, decision-making, and personalized treatment. In drug discovery, AI can accelerate target identification, support in silico compound design, and predict the effectiveness and safety p
papers.summary: This review discusses how artificial intelligence methods, including machine learning, deep learning, and large language models, are being applied across dermatology to accelerate drug discovery, improve diagnosis, and enable personalized treatment pathways. The authors highlight AI's potential to streamline target identification, compound design, and safety prediction for skin disease therapeutics.

papers.title: Nucleo-amino acid derived AMO as a potential JAK inhibitor: machine learning screening, docking, molecular dynamics, ADMET/toxicity, and MM-PBSA analysis.
papers.abstract: L-amino acids and nucleotide bases are essential biomolecules in all living organisms and represent attractive building blocks for designing safer, more effective drugs. In this study, we designed JAK kinase inhibitor candidates derived from these natural compounds and evaluated them using an integrated computational workflow. MarvinSketch was used to generate 100 structures, which were optimized in Spartan; frequency calculations confirmed the absence of imaginary modes and enabled calculation of key physicochemical properties. The candidates were then screened against five known JAK inhibito
papers.summary: This study computationally designed and screened 100 potential JAK inhibitors derived from natural amino acids and nucleotide bases, using machine learning, docking, and molecular dynamics simulations to identify promising candidates. The integrated workflow predicted several compounds with favorable binding and safety profiles, suggesting these natural building blocks could be a source for safer kinase inhibitors.

papers.title: Exploring drug repurposing for monkeypox virus: A structural and computational approach.
papers.abstract: The increasing global threat posed by viral infections that are transmitted to humans from animals has intensified interest in emerging pathogens such as monkeypox virus (MPV). This study investigated the potential of drug repurposing as an efficient therapeutic strategy against MPV, focusing on its close genetic relationships with vaccinia and variola viruses.
papers.journal: Journal of Taibah University Medical Sciences
papers.summary: This study explores drug repurposing as a strategy to treat monkeypox virus by leveraging its genetic similarity to vaccinia and variola viruses. Using structural and computational methods, the authors identify existing drugs that may be effective against MPV, offering a faster path to treatment than developing new drugs from scratch.

papers.title: T-pGNN4DTI: Towards better drug-target interactions prediction using Global Self-attentive Pooled Graph Convolutional Networks and protein pre-training Models.
papers.abstract: Identification of drug-target interactions (DTI) is an important and challenging task in drug discovery and development. Traditional methods generally require biological experiments, which are costly and time-consuming. Machine learning-based methods can rapidly predict DTI using only computer algorithmic models, allowing researchers to validate only the most promising interactions through biochemical experiments. This holds promise for effectively addressing the current challenges of lengthy development cycles and high costs in new drug development. However, it is difficult for the existing D
papers.summary: This paper presents T-pGNN4DTI, a new machine learning method that combines graph neural networks with protein pre-training models to predict drug-target interactions more accurately. The approach uses a global self-attentive pooling mechanism to better capture important features from drug and protein structures, potentially reducing the need for costly biological experiments in drug discovery.

papers.title: International perspectives on AI-driven pharmaceutical IP challenges.
papers.abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is transforming the pharmaceutical sector by optimizing care delivery and accelerating drug design. While AI introduces significant benefits, it presents complex challenges regarding intellectual property (IP) frameworks. This review examines AI-driven innovations in personalized medicine and drug design, with a specific focus on inventorship issues that directly affect pharmaceutical patent protection. We analyze recent United States (US) legal developments concerning AI inventorship, including the US Supreme Court's March 2026 denial of certiorari in Thaler v. Pe
papers.summary: This review examines how AI is transforming drug design and personalized medicine, focusing on the intellectual property challenges it creates, particularly around inventorship for patents. It analyzes recent US legal developments, including the Supreme Court's 2026 decision in Thaler v. Pe, which have implications for pharmaceutical patent protection.

papers.title: GraphTransDTI: A novel hybrid framework combining graph transformer and CNN-BiLSTM for enhanced Drug-Protein Interaction prediction.
papers.abstract: Drug-protein interaction (DTI) prediction is a pivotal step in the drug discovery and repurposing process, helping to minimize experimental costs and time. However, existing deep learning methods often face limitations in simultaneously capturing the spatial structure of drug molecules and the deep contextual correlation with protein sequences. To address this issue, we propose GraphTransDTI, a synergistic hybrid framework that integrates a Graph Transformer to represent drug graph structures, a CNN-BiLSTM network to encode protein sequence context, and a Cross-Attention mechanism to model cro
papers.summary: GraphTransDTI is a new computational method that combines graph transformers and CNN-BiLSTM networks to better predict how drugs interact with proteins. By simultaneously capturing the 3D structure of drug molecules and the sequence context of proteins, it improves prediction accuracy over existing deep learning approaches.

papers.title: AI-driven drug reposition for pathogens: a new paradigm in pandemic preparedness.
papers.abstract: Faced with a severe outbreak of diseases caused by newly emerging and recurrent pathogens, the development cycle of traditional drugs is long, making it difficult to meet emergency needs. Drug repositioning has become a key strategy for rapidly providing therapies by exploring new therapeutic uses of approved drugs. However, traditional reposition methods have bottlenecks such as slow speed and strong randomness. Artificial intelligence (AI) is revolutionizing drug reposition by analyzing and integrating multi-source data with computational models, dramatically accelerating the discovery proce
papers.summary: This review discusses how AI-driven drug repositioning can accelerate pandemic preparedness by rapidly identifying new uses for existing drugs against emerging pathogens. It highlights how AI overcomes the limitations of traditional drug repositioning methods, which are slow and random, by integrating multi-source data with computational models.

papers.title: Computer-aided drug discovery: historical foundations, practical AI tools, and emerging ethical considerations.
papers.abstract: Computer-aided drug discovery (CADD) has become an integral component of modern drug development, supporting hit identification, lead optimisation, and candidate refinement across both academia and industry. Over 4 decades of methodological progress have contributed to the discovery or optimisation of several approved therapeutics, establishing CADD as a crucial element of drug research. In parallel, recent advances in artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) have introduced a new generation of practical tools that offer improved predictive performance, accessible software implem
papers.summary: This review covers the historical development of computer-aided drug discovery (CADD) and its role in approved therapeutics, then surveys modern AI/ML tools that improve predictive performance and accessibility. It also discusses emerging ethical considerations in the field.

papers.title: From genomes to interventions: computational strategies transforming parasitology.
papers.abstract: In silico techniques have become essential in contemporary parasitology, offering swift, cost-effective, and scalable methods for exploring parasite biology, host-parasite interactions, drug resistance, the discovery of diagnostic markers, vaccine development, and the prioritization of therapeutic targets. Computational frameworks that encompass genomics, pangenomics, phylogenetics, transcriptomics, proteomics, structural biology, molecular docking, molecular dynamics simulations, artificial intelligence (AI), and immunoinformatics have collectively revolutionized parasite research. They enabl
papers.summary: This review highlights how computational methods—including genomics, AI, and structural biology—are transforming parasitology by enabling faster, cheaper, and more scalable studies of parasite biology, drug resistance, and vaccine development. These in silico approaches are now essential for discovering diagnostic markers and prioritizing therapeutic targets.

papers.title: Computational evaluation of selected compounds as potential inhibitors of gastric H⁺/K⁺-ATPase using molecular docking, molecular dynamics simulations, MMGBSA, and ADMET analyses.
papers.abstract: This study investigated the inhibitory potential of selected phytochemicals and conventional drugs against gastric H⁺/K⁺-ATPase, an important therapeutic target associated with acid-related gastrointestinal disorders such as peptic ulcers and gastroesophageal reflux disease. Computational approaches, including molecular docking, molecular dynamics simulations, MMGBSA free energy calculations, and ADMET profiling, were employed to evaluate the pharmacological relevance of the selected compounds. Molecular docking analysis revealed that several compounds exhibited favorable interactions with the
papers.summary: This study used computational methods to evaluate how well certain natural compounds and existing drugs might inhibit gastric H⁺/K⁺-ATPase, a key target for treating acid-related stomach disorders. The analysis identified several compounds with promising binding interactions and favorable drug-like properties.

papers.title: Computational QSAR and structure-based identification of plerixafor-derived PIM-1 kinase inhibitors in diffuse large B-Cell lymphoma.
papers.abstract: Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) is the most common and aggressive subtype of non-Hodgkin lymphoma, with a substantial proportion of patients developing resistance to standard chemotherapy. Chromosomal translocations resulting in overexpression of the serine/threonine kinase Proviral Integration site for Moloney murine leukemia virus (PIM-1) contribute to disease progression, therapeutic resistance, and poor clinical outcomes, establishing PIM-1 as a promising molecular target. Unlike many kinases, the ATP-binding site of PIM-1 lacks a backbone hydrogen bond donor within the hinge region
papers.summary: This study uses computational methods (QSAR and structure-based modeling) to identify plerixafor-derived compounds as potential PIM-1 kinase inhibitors for treating diffuse large B-cell lymphoma. The work targets PIM-1, a kinase overexpressed in chemotherapy-resistant DLBCL, by exploiting its unique ATP-binding site lacking a hinge region hydrogen bond donor.

papers.title: Uncovering antioxidant and anti-breast cancer potential metabolite from streptomyces strain sp. VITAMB using integrated experimental and computational approaches.
papers.abstract: Actinobacteria's are rich in bioactive compounds, showing antimicrobial, antiviral, antioxidative, anti-inflammatory, and anticancer activity. The increasing demand for these metabolites in the culinary, pharmaceutical, cosmetic, and nutraceutical industries emphasizes the latent potential of actinomycetes. Paddy field ecosystem provides unique physicochemical conditions that favor the growth of actinomycetes, making them a valuable reservoir of pigment-producing microorganisms. In the present study, a yellow pigment-producing strain identified as Streptomyces sp (GenBank ID: OR958670) was iso
papers.summary: This study identifies a yellow pigment-producing Streptomyces strain from paddy soil that produces metabolites with antioxidant and anti-breast cancer activity, using both lab experiments and computational modeling. The findings highlight agricultural soils as a source of potentially useful bioactive compounds for pharmaceutical development.

papers.title: MetaphorPrompt2-A Structure and Function-Focused Approach for Extracting Causal Events from Biological Text.
papers.abstract: Extracting molecular regulatory pathways (MRPs) from biomedical literature is crucial for building knowledge graphs that represent disease mechanisms and molecular regulation. However, even advanced large language models (LLMs) using in-context learning often misinterpret complex domain-specific causal statements or omit intermediary steps, resulting in incomplete pathway representations. MetaphorPrompt2 is motivated by cognitive theories of structural mapping and causal event representation. It improves MRP extraction by emphasizing structural relations and functional roles of biological enti
papers.summary: This paper introduces MetaphorPrompt2, a method that improves the extraction of molecular regulatory pathways from biomedical text by focusing on structural relations and functional roles of biological entities. The approach addresses limitations of large language models that often misinterpret complex causal statements or miss intermediate steps in pathway representations.

papers.title: AI-driven molecular diversification and ligand-based optimization of macitentan derivatives targeting VEGFR1 and endothelin signaling pathways.
papers.abstract: Preeclampsia is a hypertensive disorder of pregnancy marked by angiogenic imbalance and endothelial dysfunction, driven largely by over expression of soluble fms-like tyrosine kinase-1 (sFlt-1) and up-regulation of endothelin-1 (ET-1). Targeting both VEGFR1 and ET-1 receptor could offer a dual-action therapeutic approach. In this study, macitentan, a dual ETA/ ETB antagonist, was used as a scaffold for AI-assisted derivative generation aimed at dual inhibition of these receptors. A comprehensive computational pipeline was applied including molecular docking, pharmacophore modeling, molecular d
papers.summary: This study uses AI to generate and optimize derivatives of the drug macitentan, aiming to create a dual-action therapy for preeclampsia by targeting both VEGFR1 and endothelin receptors. The computational pipeline, including molecular docking and pharmacophore modeling, identified promising candidates for further development.
